locator_text: Chapter 2, discussion of the Amir Nezam Letgahi family
quoted_text: The Amir Nezam Letgahi family is traced through Abdollah Khan, son of Mostafa Qoli Khan E'temad al-Saltaneh, son of Mohammad Baqer Khan, son of Haji Mohammad Khan Jeyhunabadi; Mohtaj Ali Khan and Mansur Ali Khan are named as his sons.
Letgah is identified as the family's main center, and Kabudarahang as another important landed area.
The user's father belongs under Gholamhossein Khan, not Gholam Ali; Gholam Ali and Gholamhossein are brothers.

locator_text: User visual correction of family-tree figure
quoted_text: Mansur Ali Khan has no descendants shown in the figure.
Abdollah Khan was appointed governor of Khuzestan; Hossein Qoli Khan received Sa'ed al-Saltaneh and Mohtaj Ali Khan received Ejlal al-Mamalek.
Mohtaj Ali Khan Ejlal al-Mamalek Amir Arfa' and Mansur Ali Khan Sardar Akram are identified as sons of Abdollah Khan.
The index lists Mohtaj Ali Khan Ejlal al-Mamalek Amir Arfa' on pages 61 and 152.

locator_text: Chapter 2 genealogy of the Ashiqloo branch
quoted_text: Mohammad Hossein Khan had five sons, including Nabi Khan Gharagozloo.
Thousands of Gharagozloo troops served in the Herat campaign under Nabi Khan; Abdollah Khan, holding the rank of yavar, supported his troops.
In 1255 AH British forces attacked Bushehr; fifty Gharagozloo soldiers guarded the port, but the small Iranian force could not prevent occupation.
Abdollah Khan was wounded and consequently obtained the rank of sarhang; Gharagozloo troops worked to capture the fortress and open its gates.
Shir Ali Khan repeatedly climbed the fortress wall and attempted to plant the victory flag; he received a robe of honor and decoration.
Rashid Khan, a colonel of the Mokhberan regiment, was wounded in the leg; Nabi Khan was severely wounded and died shortly afterward.
By order of Mohammad Shah, Nabi Khan and other Gharagozloo dead were transferred for burial in Mashhad.
Mahmoud Khan was the son of Nabi Khan and a descendant of Mohammad Hossein Khan Ashiqloo; after Nabi Khan’s death at Herat Mohammad Shah issued a decree concerning him.
Mahmoud Khan advocated peace; Mostafa Qoli Khan Mirpanj attempted to stop the British ship at Bandar Lengeh and exchanged fire before withdrawing inland.
Rustam Khan and his son Ali Khan joined Iranian forces against the British; Ali Khan suffered a superficial arm wound and three Gharagozloo soldiers were captured.
Ali Naqi Khan defeated Nasrallah Khan in Bikhuh; Ali Khan Nosrat al-Molk and Lotf Ali Khan served with Gharagozloo troops in Kerman.
Mohammad Hossein Khan, son of Ali Khan, was granted the title Hosam al-Molk in 1287 AH for service in Kerman.
Zeyn al-Abedin Khan married his son Gholamreza Khan Ehtesham al-Saltaneh to Qamar al-Saltaneh in 1309 AH in a costly month-long celebration.
Hosam al-Molk, Saed al-Saltaneh, and Zia al-Molk were blamed for unrest, dismissed, and detained in 1310 AH.
Hosam al-Molk offered very large payments to obtain provincial governments and was ultimately appointed governor of Kermanshah on 12 Rabi I 1311 AH.
Aqa Jan Gharagozloo, a yavar, fled after Pavlov was killed in an altercation with his servant Hasan Tupchi; he was arrested in Hamadan and delivered to the Russian embassy.

locator_text: Late Qajar military decline and Shiraz mission
quoted_text: The role of tribal regiments declined; Gharagozloo forces under Saed al-Saltaneh were sent against unrest in Shiraz in Shawwal 1311 AH, with seven or eight deaths.
Mahmoud Khan, son of Nabi Khan, inherited his father’s command, served as deputy governor in Hamadan, and received several district governments.
After Nabi Khan’s death, Mahmoud Khan was appointed colonel in 1254 AH and promoted to sartip in 1255 AH before service in Khorasan.

locator_text: Mahmoud Khan diplomatic and ministerial career
quoted_text: Mahmoud Khan served as envoy to Russia, returned as mirpanj, and later held senior ministries and ceremonial diplomatic assignments.
